other_observers: none; Cole Wild reported the bird the night before.
duration: two occasions about 10 minutes apart, a couple minutes long each
specific_location: Along the north shore of a small retention pond in the Big Thompson River drainage, just east of Wilson Ave, south of the paved trail but north of the Loveland recycling center.
description: The bird was a small passerine moving frequently through the tangle of branches of Russian Olives among Yellow-rumped Warblers, but I saw its distinctly yellow underparts, black bib, and gray head with the slight suggestion of a dark mask. There were no eye arcs or eye rings, just a dark shading around the eye and seemed to me to include the lores. I didn't observe much else about the bird after my initial identification, as I focused more on getting a photograph of it while I could.Behaviors: The bird was very active, moving frequently from one branch to the next in a tree, and moving from tree to tree in a methodical manner seemingly with several other warblers, all Yellow-rumped and Orange-crowned as best as I could tell.Call: sharp, loud chip notes given every second or two; no song heard. these call notes stood out from those of the other birds present in the area that morning, including other warblers (Yellow-rumped, Yellow, and Orange-crowned) and chickadees, etc.Plumage: alternate
